text: The surgeon uses robotic instruments to take down inflammatory adhesions between the omentum and the gallbladder, elevating the gallbladder for better visualization. The bottom of the gallbladder is visible, and the surgeon identifies the cystic duct before beginning dissection at the neck of the gallbladder.

The surgeon carefully opens the peritoneum, navigating through thickened tissue to locate the artery medially. A window is created behind a structure suspected to be the artery, but dissection continues without clipping. The stone in the gallbladder neck is milked upwards, improving elevation of the gallbladder and visibility of the cystic duct. The surgeon continues dissection to open the triangle, with the stone's position aiding in visualization of the gallbladder wall.

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct and confirms its location, then proceeds to dissect the artery free from surrounding fatty tissue on the medial aspect of the gallbladder. A window is created behind the artery, and the surgeon works within the triangle to achieve the critical view, lifting the bottom of the gallbladder for better access.

The surgeon creates a window behind the artery, which does not enhance with ICG, confirming its identity. Clips are placed on the artery, and it is transected despite minor bleeding, which is controlled. The surgeon then focuses on dissecting the cystic duct, clearing inflamed fibrous and fatty tissue around it. The dissection plane between the gallbladder and liver bed is fibrotic, complicating the creation of a window. Tissue is thinned over the duct, and slow cauterization is used to avoid damaging potential vessels. Bile begins to egress from the cut ends as tension is released.

The surgeon identifies a hole in the gallbladder with purulent bile leaking out. They close the holes on the gallbladder and cystic duct to prevent further bile leakage, which interferes with ICG usage. The surgeon cautiously examines the area to ensure no additional ductal structures are present, while a partner observes the procedure. The surgeon continues dissection, becoming more confident that the cystic duct has been transected and there is no further ductal structure posteriorly.

The surgeon encounters bleeding while removing the gallbladder from the liver bed, using bipolar cautery to control it. The gallbladder tissue is thicker than anticipated, prompting repeated use of ICG to ensure no bile leakage from ductal structures. The surgeon inspects the area thoroughly for any signs of bile leakage.

The surgeon performs a top-down dissection near the scarred upper area of the gallbladder. After extensive irrigation, they carefully inspect the site for over fifteen minutes, confirming no further bile leakage from the stitched area. The cystic duct appears to have avulsed from the common bile duct, which is visible lower down. The surgeon applies pressure to the bile duct to check for leakage but observes none, achieving hemostasis and irrigating further.

The surgeon performs a final inspection of the surgical site, confirming no leakage from the dissected structures. A drain is placed, and the gallbladder bed is observed for any signs of bleeding or bile leakage.
